topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The campus map, once a source of pride, now felt like a cruel joke spread across my desk. Every line, every landmark, seemed to mock the sudden, heavy silence that had settled over the administrative building. I traced the outline of the library with a trembling finger, the cheap paper crinkling under the pressure. My stomach churned, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I kept glancing at the framed photo of the quad, the vibrant green now seeming muted, faded. "It can't be true," I whispered, the sound swallowed instantly by the echoing emptiness of my small dorm room. I stood up abruptly, needing to pace, needing movement to counteract the paralyzing stillness that had taken root in my limbs.

[story 2]

I stared at the eviction notice tacked haphazardly to the community bulletin board, the stark black ink seeming to vibrate under the fluorescent lights. My hands were slick with sweat as I reread the final paragraph, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. A deep, shaky breath hitched in my throat, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the reality unfolding before me. The air in the hallway felt thick, suffocating. When I finally lowered my hands, my gaze drifted to the worn copy of poetry I kept tucked in my backpack; the familiar words offered no comfort, only a hollow echo. I wanted to turn around and run, but my feet felt cemented to the linoleum floor.

[story 3]

Professor Davies’ announcement still looped in my mind, the polite, measured tone grating against the sudden, sharp anxiety clawing at my chest. I sat on the edge of my bed, meticulously stacking my textbooks into perfect, rigid towers, a desperate attempt to impose order on the chaos churning inside. Each book felt heavy, a tangible weight pressing down on my shoulders. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but my thumb hovered over the dial button, paralyzed by the fear of hearing the same news echoed back. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot path down my cheek, quickly followed by a desperate swipe of the sleeve across my face. The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic drumming of my own pulse.
